coredata.dc:description: Constantly escalating oxidative stress greatly increases the daily demand, and total antioxidant capacity (TAC) have become an important indicator. Therefore, the development of effective methods for TAC evaluation remains to be a research hotspot. Herein, a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on Fe-adsorbing carbon dots nanozymes (Fe-CDs) was constructed through a rational design. Fe-adsorbing step quenched the fluorescence of carbon dots and restored it by adding antioxidants to achieve fluorescence detection. Furthermore, it endowed Fe-CDs with peroxidase (POD) activity necessary for achieving colorimetric detection. A dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array based on Fe-CDs was further constructed to selectively recognize 7 antioxidants and accurately evaluate TAC in 5 actual samples. In addition, Fe-CDs demonstrated excellent accuracy and anti-interference performance in detecting ascorbic acid TAC in fruit juice based on fluorescence/colorimetric methods, with detection limits of 2.1 and 0.4 μM as well as linear ranges of 4–40 and 6.3–150 μM, respectively. Moreover, Fe-CDs indeed guided changes in glutathione content through fluorescence imaging at the cellular level

head.ce:title: Fluorescence/colorimetric intelligent sensor based on Fe-adsorbing carbon dots nanozymes for selective recognition and quantification of total antioxidant capacity in foods and cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constantly escalating oxidative stress greatly increases the daily demand, and total antioxidant capacity (TAC) have become an important indicator. Therefore, the development of effective methods for TAC evaluation remains to be a research hotspot. Herein, a fluorescence/colorimetric sensor based on Fe-adsorbing carbon dots nanozymes (Fe-CDs) was constructed through a rational design. Fe-adsorbing step quenched the fluorescence of carbon dots and restored it by adding antioxidants to achieve fluorescence detection. Furthermore, it endowed Fe-CDs with peroxidase (POD) activity necessary for achieving colorimetric detection. A dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array based on Fe-CDs was further constructed to selectively recognize 7 antioxidants and accurately evaluate TAC in 5 actual samples. In addition, Fe-CDs demonstrated excellent accuracy and anti-interference performance in detecting ascorbic acid TAC in fruit juice based on fluorescence/colorimetric methods, with detection limits of 2.1 and 0.4 μM as well as linear ranges of 4–40 and 6.3–150 μM, respectively. Moreover, Fe-CDs indeed guided changes in glutathione content through fluorescence imaging at the cellular level

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antioxidants are electron or hydrogen donors in redox reactions. They regulate oxidative stress induced by reactive oxygen species in the body, thereby improving the body's natural antioxidant defense system (Zhu et al., 2024). Many studies have shown that foods rich in antioxidants can reduce oxidative stress reactions, greatly reducing the risk of diseases caused by oxidative stress (Fan et al., 2025; Hata, Surek, Sartori, Serrato, & Spinosa, 2023; Zhao et al., 2024). Therefore, effective detection of total antioxidant capacity (TAC) in foods and cells has important physiological significance. Moreover, quantitative detection of antioxidants facilitates quality evaluation of related foods. So far, many analytical methods have been developed to detect TAC, such as electrochemical methods (Iranmanesh, Foroughi, Jahani, Zandi, & Nadiki, 2020; Verma, Thakur, Pandey, & Kumar, 2023), spectroscopic methods including colorimetry (Bagdeli, Kajani, & Taheri-Kafrani, 2024; Wang et al., 2023; S. Z. Li, Chu, Xia, Wei, & Lu, 2024), fluorescence (Luo et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2025; Zheng et al., 2023), chemiluminescence (Wu et al., 2023), and nanoparticle based methods (Deng, Ren, Zhang, Wang, & Lu, 2024; Eryaman et al., 2024; Li et al., 2025; Li et al., 2025). However, the forementioned methods for TAC detection only output a single signal, which is susceptible to interference and reduces the accuracy of the results. More importantly, these techniques cannot accurately analyze individual antioxidants without considering the possible interactions between them. Therefore, it is necessary to design and develop selective, sensitive, and reliable sensors for TAC detection in clinical practice, food industry, and environmental monitoring.

The sensor combination of fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode utilizes various spectral methods with different principles. The output of two different light signals for self-correction and self-verification serves as a faster, reliable, and accurate sensor platform (J. Y. Li, Gu, Jin, Sun, & Er, 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Yue et al., 2024; Ali, Hassan, Hassan, & El-Wekil, 2021). Interestingly, the fluorescent/colorimetric dual-mode sensor can be used to calculate and analyze the cross-response results between two sensing units to construct a sensor array (Liu et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2022). Through the dual-mode signal output (fluorescence and colorimetry), it overcomes the limitations of traditional single analyte antioxidant determination by cross-validation and matrix interference correction based solely on one type of nanomaterial, which successfully addresses the problem of traditional detection methods for distinguishing antioxidants in TAC. As an intelligent method, the sensor array is not a specific “lock key” sensor; rather, it uses nonspecific recognition elements to generate complex response patterns (unique fingerprint of each analyte) demonstrating reliability and practicality (F. L. Wu et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2020; F. L. Wu et al., 2025). Statistical algorithms, such as linear discriminant analysis, principal component analysis (PCA), and hierarchical cluster analysis, are used to calculate the cross-response results between multiple sensing units and analytes, providing a universal method for the identification of multiple analytes in TAC (Ni, Li, Yan, Huang, & Zou, 2024; Shi et al., 2024). However, this development, which combines recognition and quantification, remains relatively limited, particularly for intelligent sensors that effectively integrate fluorescence/colorimetric method.

Nanozymes are a type of simulated enzyme with unique properties and catalytic functions of nanomaterials, characterized by high catalytic efficiency, good stability, and large-scale preparation (Sheng et al., 2024). Recently, the antioxidant performance of different compounds has been evaluated by utilizing the inhibitory effect of antioxidants on the catalytic activity of nanozymes with (POD) activity. TAC can be determined by quantifying the measured color intensity in the presence and absence of antioxidants in the sample, demonstrating the enormous potential of nanozymes as valuable tools for TAC evaluation and further analytical applications (Li et al., 2023; Lou, Zhao, Wang, & Wei, 2019; Wang et al., 2023). Among them, Fe(II) and Fe(III) are the most commonly used metal ions in the construction of POD-activity nanozymes (Alqahtani, Mahmoud, & El-Wekil, 2023). At the same time, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention for their superior properties such as easy preparation and surface functionalization, stable optical properties, and good biocompatibility, and their applications in biological imaging, as well as drug/gene delivery (T. T. Li et al., 2024; Liao et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2024). When the surface of CDs is loaded with metal cations (Fe3+, Cu2+, etc.), it induces significant fluorescence quenching (Shan et al., 2022; J. Li et al., 2022; Mahmoud, El-Wekil, Ali, Batakoushy, & Shahin, 2022). And after interaction with antioxidants, it restores its own fluorescence, enabling rapid and accurate detection of TAC through another fluorescence method. Therefore, Fe-adsorbing CDs nanozymes have become a promising candidate for designing intelligent fluorescence/colorimetric sensors to selectively and accurately recognize and quantify TAC. Unfortunately, the idea of killing two birds with one stone based on Fe-adsorbing CDs nanozymes has not been further developed and applied.

Herein, we constructed an Fe-adsorbing CDs nanozymes (Fe-CDs) to achieve simple, and reliable dual-mode antioxidant detection method. Fe-adsorbing carbon dots enhanced POD-like activity and adjusted the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, as shown in Scheme 1 . Multiple antioxidants with different effectes on color fading of oxidized TMB (oxTMB) and fluorescence recovery of CDs, achieving different spectral changes in two independent sensor units and unique fluorescence/colorimetric responses to the corresponding antioxidants. Subsequently, Fe-CDs sensor array was proposed to successfully identify 7 antioxidants and 5 fruits, as well as to detect the accurate content or dynamic changes of typical antioxidants (AA or GSH) in fruit juices and cancer cells.

Unless otherwise stated, all compounds and reagents were purchased and used directly without purification. Citric acid (≥99.5 %), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, ≥99 %(HPLC)), Glutathione (GSH, ≥98 %), N-Ethylmaleimide (NEM, ≥98 %) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 97 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Benzoylurea (98 %) and Acetic acid (AA, 98 %) was purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd. Iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3•6H2O, 99 %) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) were purchased from China Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. and used directly without further purification. Gallic acid (GA, 99 %) and Tannic acid (TA, 99 %) were purchased from Damas Beta. Sodium sulfide (Na2S, AR), Cysteine (Cys, 97 %) and Caffeic acid (CA, 97 %) were purchased from Bide Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. The resistivity of deionized water used in the experiment was 18 MΩ cm−1. Both 1640 medium and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Invitrogen Corporation (USA). The HL-7702 cells and A549 cell lines were obtained from the Shanghai Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Fruit juices were obtained from the local supermarket, respectively.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) images were obtained via a Zeiss sigma 300. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) were characterized by Haoyuan Instrument DX-2800. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 FTIR spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured on a Waters TGA-550 analyzer. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were characterized by a CIQTEK EPR200 Spectroscopy. Zeta potential was measured on a BeNano 180. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer (UV-1900) and a HORIBA FlouroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Record cell imaging using a confocal laser PL microscope (Zeiss LSM 780).

Briefly, 1.92 g of citric acid and 1.08 g of benzoylurea were mixed and transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave without adding any solvent. After 10 h of pyrolysis reaction at 220 °C, the cooled solid residue was added to 20 mL of DI water and dialyzed against DI water with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) for 2 days. Finally, the CDs dispersion was freeze-dried to obtain CDs as a brown solid and stored at room temperature for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Fe3+ adsorption on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of FeCl₃·6H₂O solutions (1–1000 μM) were added to a 100 μg/mL CDs dispersion. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs dispersion was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer after 2 min, and excitation wavelength was 365 nm. Additionally, the time-dependent fluorescence response (2–30 min) was investigated at a fixed FeCl₃·6H₂O concentration of 50 μM under the same measurement conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe-CDs carbon dots nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μM FeCl₃·6H₂O solution and 100 μg/mL CDs dispersion were added to a tube. The mixture was allowed to interact for 1 min, after which the solution was transferred into a dialysis tube and dialyzed for 24 h. The resulting Fe-CDs were freeze-dried and stored at room temperature in a dry environment until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array for selective recognition antioxidants and foods
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array was used to distinguish antioxidants, enabling the detection and identification of 7 antioxidants (AA, Cys, TA, GSH, CA, GA, and Na2S). For the fluorescence sensor, 50 μM of antioxidants solution were added to 500 μg/mL Fe-CDs for 10 min. The fluorescence intensity at 429 nm was measured. For the colorimetric sensor, the same antioxidants solution was added to oxTMB system (500 μg/mL Fe-CDs + 0.5 mg/mL TMB + 0.3 % H2O2 in pH 3.5 buffer, pre-incubated for 60 min), and followed by an additional 2 min incubation before analysis.

Sea-buckthorn, apple, mango, pineapple, orange was collected and juiced. Then, the 5 fruit juices were centrifugated for 3 times, and established sensor array was employed to discriminate and determination fruit juices. The original data of the matrix was processed to PCA in Origin 2022 software, yielding visually distinguishable clustering results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA TAC in juice by fluorescence/colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of AA by fluorescence sensor, different concentrations of AA solution (0–40 μM) were added to 100 μg/mL Fe-CDs and incubated for 2 min. The fluorescence intensity at 429 nm was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. For the detection of AA by colorimetric sensor, different concentrations of AA solution (0–150 μM) were added to another oxTMB system (1 mg/mL Fe-CDs + 0.5 mg/mL TMB + 0.3 % H2O2 in pH 3.5 buffer, pre-incubated 30 min) and reacted for 2 min before measuring absorbance at 650 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as LOD = 3σ/k, where σ represents the standard deviation of the experiment and k is the slope of the linear curve.

Spiked recovery experiments were performed by adding standard AA solutions (10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM for fluorescence analysis, and 50 μM, 100 μM for colorimetric analysis). Recovery percentages were calculated by comparing measured values to the expected concentrations using established calibration curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference assay of the fluorescence/colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the anti-interference capability of the sensor, various amino acids (Ala, Glu, His, Leu, Met, Phe, Pro, Ser, Thr, Trp, Val, Tyr, Asp), and salts (MgSO4, KCl, NaCl, ZnSO4, CaCl2, KBr) at 2 mM were added to each 100 μg/mL Fe-CDs or oxTMB system in a 96 well plate. The fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and the absorbance at 650 nm was measured for the colorimetric method using an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) reader.
A549 cells and HL-7702 cells were cultured in 1640 medium with 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics (penicillin–streptomycin, 10 kU/mL) maintained in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH content in cells
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells at 5 × 104 cells per well were incubated for 12 h before dividing into four groups. The group 1: Untreated cells (control); The group 2: Treated with 100 μg/mL Fe-CDs; The group 3: Pretreated with 1 mM GSH for 20 min (washed with medium), and then treated with 100 μg/mL Fe-CDs; The group 4: Pretreated with 1 mM GSH for 20 min, then exposed to 1 mM N-ethylmaleimide (NEM) before 100 μg/mL Fe-CDs treatment. After all the treatment, the 4 groups were incubated for another 4 h, washed with PBS, and imaged by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm.

Normal human liver cells (HL-7702 cells) were seeded into 96-well plates at 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. Different concentrations of Fe-CDs (12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) were added to each well and cultured for 24 h for the MTT assay.
Unless otherwise stated, all results were obtained from at least triplicate and expressed as the mean values ± standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, Fe-adsorbing performance optimization and characterization of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the present study, drawing inspiration from the interaction between CDs and iron ions, multifunctional groups functionalized CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis method. Through the large specific surface area and functional groups of CDs (Campuzano, Yáñez-Sedeño, & Pingarrón, 2019; Durán, Simoes, de Moraes, Fávaro, & Seabra, 2016), Fe (III) could be effectively adsorbed, leading to easy acquisition of Fe-adsorbing carbon dots nanozymes (Fe-CDs) driven by electrostatic interactions and coordination bonding between Fe3+ and the aforementioned functional groups. Here, CDs as fluorescence sensors endowed Fe-CDs with fluorescence detection properties, whereas Fe as a source of POD-activity endowed it with colorimetric detection ability. Based on this design concept, intelligent fluorescence/colorimetric sensor for detecting antioxidant capacity had been successfully constructed. To validate the above design, different concentrations of FeCl3 were added to explore the optimal additional concentration for CDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, when FeCl3 was gradually added, the fluorescence intensity of CDs markedly decreased to quench the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Further quantification of the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at 429 nm in Fig. 1B to seek the optimal addition ratio, it was found that the fluorescence intensity had good linear consistency (R2 = 0.9817) at Fe-adsorbing concentrations ranging from 0 to 50 μM in Fig. S1. Therefore, we chose the above conditions as the optimal ratio for the Fe-adsorbing process. The reaction time of Fe-adsorbing was further examined in Fig. 1C. Over time, the fluorescence intensity of CDs rapidly decreased in the first 2 min and reached equilibrium after approximately 20 min. Meanwhile, to achieve more thorough Fe-adsorbing effect, 1 h was selected as the final reaction time. The Fe-adsorbing stability of this strategy was also detected via inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry (ICP–MS) for the release of Fe from Fe-CDs. After 12 h, the total release of Fe in the system remained less than the limit of detection (LOD = 10 ppm). This also fully demonstrated the extremely strong stability of the Fe-adsorbing construction strategy.

Benefiting from the abundant −OH, −NH2 and − COOH structures in carbon source, Fe-CDs were easily formed and their corresponding structures were characterized. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results indicated that Fe-CDs exhibited uniform nanodots and had a lattice fringe with a mean distance of 0.230 nm, which was consistent with those of unabsorbed CDs as shown in Fig. S2. Subsequently, the particle-size distribution was calculated (Fig. S3) using the average particle sizes of CDs and Fe-CDs being 4.69 and 9.20 nm, respectively. The phenomenon of increased particle size of CDs after Fe-adsorbing was mainly owing to the coordination crosslinking between Fe and surface functional groups of CDs, resulting in multiple CDs bridging through Fe and forming small aggregates. In addition, the surface functional chemical groups of Fe-CDs were further analyzed via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. 1D). The absorption at approximately 3433 cm−1 indicated OH or NH stretching vibration, the absorption peak at approximately 2900–3000 cm−1 indicated CH stretching vibration, and the absorption at 1186 cm−1 indicated CO stretching vibration. The CO and CONH bonds mainly appeared near 1708 cm−1, the CN bond was approximately at 1666 cm−1, and a CN characteristic absorption peak at 1407 cm−1 (Shan et al., 2021). Compared to CDs, the Fe element allowed coordination with the −OH, −NH2, and − COOH structures in CDs. An absorption band near 1500 cm−1 was observed, which represented the characteristic stretching vibration peak of −COO− and indicated the coordination between −COOH and Fe. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peaks of FeO or FeN were observed in the range of 723 and 633 cm−1 (Pan et al., 2025; Yuan, Sun, Zhang, Shi, & Guo, 2024). This initially confirmed the successful coordination and adsorption between CDs and Fe to achieve the construction of Fe-CDs (Liao et al., 2022). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum as shown in Fig. 1E confirmed the presence of Fe and Cl in Fe-CDs. The valence states of Fe in Fe-CDs included Fe(II) and Fe(III) at a ratio of 7:4 (Fig. 1F), which indicated that CDs might reduce Fe(III) to Fe(II) during the Fe-adsorbing process. The high-resolution XPS spectrum as depicted in Fig. S4 shows a peak from 282 to 290 eV, which could be attributed to the C–C/C=C, CN, C=O/C–O bond. The N 1 s spectrum showed subpeaks corresponding to amino-N and pyrrole/pyridine-N bonds at around 400 eV, and the O 1 s spectrum showed subpeaks corresponding to the O=C–O, CO, and CO bonds from 528 to 535 eV, respectively. Since the addition of a small amount of Fe does not significantly affect the crystal structure of CDs (Chen et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2021). An X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed the crystallinity of Fe-CDs with matched main peak at 22.4° to CDs in Fig. 1G, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of carbon materials (Bai et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2024). This broad peak indicated that CDs and Fe-CDs might have a graphene-like structure with lower crystallinity (Wu et al., 2023). The thermogravimetric (TGA) curve of Fe-CDs and CDs was shown in Fig. 1H. The first stage with an initial temperature of 100 °C was set to facilitate the evaporation of adsorbed water. TGA showed that the lower weight loss in Fe-CDs compared to CDs, which was related to the reduced hydrophilicity of Fe adsorbed with hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs. In the second stage with a temperature range of 100 °C–200 °C, Fe-CDs lost more weight, which might be due to the decomposition of ligands introduced by Fe. When the temperature reached 200 °C, the internal structure of Fe-CDs and CDs began to decompose, leading to a sharp decline in quality. The above mentioned results clarified the clarity of Fe-CDs in structural design through a simple Fe-adsorbing strategy.

The basic optical properties of CDs and Fe-CDs as fluorescence sensor had also been investigated Figs. 1I, S5 and S6. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission feature under excitation from 300 to 445 nm was also found to be attributed the surface state of Fe-CDs. Moreover, it was found that CDs had a relatively obvious UV absorption in the range of 350–375 nm, which was consistent with Fe-CDs. This indicated that Fe-adsorbing showed almost no effect on the absorbance range of CDs themselves. The 3D spectrum revealed that the optimal excitation of Fe-CDs was around 365 nm, corresponding to a maximum emission wavelength of approximately 429 nm. Fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime have also been extensively investigated in Table S1 and Fig. S7. The fluorescence quantum yields of CDs and Fe-CDs were found to be 58.02 % and 41.21 %, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime values for CDs and Fe-CDs were found to be 2.78 and 2.42 ns, respectively. The decrease in fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime could have been due to the formation of coordination bonds between Fe and oxygen−/nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CDs, which disrupted the electronic structure of the original fluorescence emitting groups. Furthermore, the optical and solution stabilities of Fe-CDs were also examined in Fig. S8. After 20 consecutive fluorescence scans, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs remained almost unchanged, with an RSD of only 0.89 %. After 7 consecutive days of room temperature storage, although the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs slightly decreased after the 3rd day, it still maintained good stability, with fluorescence intensity exceeding 90 % in the first three days. The interbatch consistency of Fe-CDs synthesis was evaluated in Fig. S9. The fluorescence intensity between 5 batches maintained good consistency, ranging from 97 % to 102 %. Based on the above data, Fe-CDs exhibited a good stability to support subsequent practical application scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification and mechanism of fluorescence/colorimetric activity of Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dual-mode sensing capability was studied using ascorbic acid (AA) as a typical antioxidant. When AA was added to Fe-CDs, the fluorescence emission peak of Fe-CDs recovered toward the original fluorescence intensity of CDs in Fig. 2A. After adding 50 μM of AA, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs markedly increased by approximately 1.5 times, which verified the potential of Fe-CDs as an intelligent fluorescence sensor for antioxidant detection and intracellular imaging. A single-blind experiment was also conducted using CDs. As shown in Figs. S10 and S11, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged before and after the addition of 50 μM AA, and the calculated difference in fluorescence intensity was 1.1 %. Furthermore, the zeta potential remained constant during this process, at −8.9 ± 1.0 mV and − 8.8 ± 0.6 mV, respectively. On the other hand, the CDs + TMB group did not show a significant increase in absorption caused by TMB oxidation, regardless of whether H2O2 was added. Meanwhile, the absorbance at 652 nm of the oxTMB system based on CDs was less than 0.1. The single-blind experiment showed that the interaction between antioxidants and CDs did not cause fluorescence intensity and absorbance changes in the absence of Fe, further demonstrating the accuracy and specificity of Fe-CDs in the dual-mode detection of antioxidants. Subsequently, the POD-like activity of Fe-CDs for colorimetric analysis was evaluated using the TMB substrate in the presence of H2O2 as shown in Fig. 2B. Fe-CDs were found to demonstrate the promising POD-like activity. Both the presence and absence of H2O2 led to the formation of a distinct oxidative TMB (oxTMB) absorption characteristic peak at 652 nm, indicating the presence of oxidase (OXD) and POD-like activity of Fe-CDs. Under the same conditions, the POD-like activity was stronger; thus, the further research on POD-like activity is warranted. When AA was present in the oxTMB system, the original blue color of oxTMB considerably faded and reduced to colorless TMB as depicted in Fig. 2B. Meanwhile, the absorbance value at 650 nm decreased, which preliminarily confirmed the potential of Fe-CDs as an intelligent colorimetric sensor for detecting antioxidants.

In principle, CDs quenched its fluorescence by Fe-adsorbing and then strongly interacted with antioxidants to restore their own fluorescence, further detecting the antioxidants through fluorescence method. Fe-adsorbing in Fe-CDs also played pivotal role in demonstrating POD-like nanozyme activity. First, the zeta potential changes before and after the reaction were examined. As shown in Fig. 2C, when AA was added, the zeta potential of Fe-CDs decreased and remained basically consistent with the original CDs. Combined with significant fluorescence enhancement, this might be a manifestation of CDs itself. XPS was employed to further investigate the above hypothesis as shown in Fig. S12. After the addition of AA, the ratio of Fe (III) to Fe (II) in Fe-CDs decreased from 7:4 to 3:2. Fe (III) in Fe-CDs reacted with antioxidants through redox reactions and converted them into Fe (II) with poor quenching ability, thereby restoring the strong blue fluorescence of CDs and achieving the purpose of antioxidant fluorescence analysis. In this TMB system, TMB was oxidized to a single electron loss oxidation state by hydroxyl radicals (OH•) generated through the Fenton reaction on the surface of nanozymes. In order to understand the POD-like nanozyme mechanism of Fe-CDs, the generated OH• were detected and analyzed using electron spin resonance (ESR) technology. When OH• were captured by the spin trapping agent DMPO, four additional peaks with the intensity ratios of 1:2:2:1 appeared. As shown in Fig. 2D, the strongest characteristic signal peak was observed only after the addition of H2O2. This confirmed that the mechanism of adding Fe to enhance the activity of POD was the successful conversion of H2O2 to OH• through the Fenton reaction, laying the foundation for subsequent colorimetric analysis of antioxidants. Based on the above data, the Fe-CDs had been proven to be a solid tool for antioxidant detection through fluorescence/colorimetric analysis, both in terms of principle and mechanism, as well as practical experiments as shown in Fig. 2E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of antioxidants by the fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array based on Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to maximize the recognition performance of various common antioxidants, the fluorescence/colorimetric intelligent sensor array based on Fe-CDs was developed. 7 antioxidants (AA, Cys, TA, GSH, CA, GA, and Na2S) were selected to detect the resolution of the sensor array. As shown in Fig. 3A, different antioxidants had different effects on the fluorescence of Fe-CDs, resulting in varying degrees of fluorescence enhancement or reduction in the fluorescence sensor unit. Among them, Na2S showed the most considerable increase in fluorescence. Notably, TA and CA not did not only enhance fluorescence, but also further quenched fluorescence. The absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs after adding TA and CA were determined in Figs. S13 and S14. Upon adding TA or CA, the absorption spectrum of the mixed system showed a decrease, whereas the fluorescence lifetime remained basically unchanged. It was highly likely that their phenol structure could further quench fluorescence through the internal filtration effect. Meanwhile, the reducing ability of different antioxidants on the oxTMB system resulted in varying degrees of inhibition on each colorimetric sensor unit in Fig. 3B. Therefore, Fe-CDs could be used to distinguish the fluorescence (ΔF = F/F0) and colorimetric (ΔI = I/I0) response for each antioxidant, based on an excellent and independent dual-mode mechanism. Moreover, a heatmap called “fingerprint” was generated for 7 antioxidants (Fig. 3C). These results indicated that this fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array was capable of selectively recognizing antioxidants. Based on PCA analysis and using these two independent discriminative factors, the training data matrix was transformed into visual classification results (Fig. 3D). Clearly, each of the 7 antioxidants could bind into an independent cluster without errors, further selectively recognizing the different types of antioxidants. Concurrently, the violin chart also proved that Fe-CDs had great value in recognition antioxidants (Fig. S15). This further explained that the fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array developed by Fe-CDs had an excellent selectively recognition ability for multiple antioxidants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TAC identification of five different types of fruit juice
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the applicability of sensor arrays in real sample systems for detection and analysis is crucial, as it is an important criterion for testing whether sensor arrays could be applied. The present study used a fluorescence/colorimetric intelligent sensor array based on Fe-CDs to evaluate the TAC quality of five different fruits (sea-buckthorn, apple, mango, pineapple, orange). PCA and system cluster analysis (SCA) were used to identify various types of fruit and evaluate the practical feasibility of fluorescence/colorimetric sensor arrays based on different TACs (Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4B, the TACs in these 5 different fruits were accurately classified without classification errors and had good selective recognition ability. The 2D standard scoring table effectively distinguished the TAC differences among the 5 fruits. In addition, a significant difference in the violin plot scores between discriminant factor 1 and fruits samples as shown in Fig. 4C was observed. SCA further indicated that these 5 fruits could be accurately classified based on the differences in TAC. Owing to their high and similar TAC, mango and orange were classified in the same category as that of pineapple (Fig. 4D). These results showed that the fluorescence/colorimetric intelligent sensor array based on Fe-CDs had the potential to accurately evaluate fruit TAC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence/colorimetric detection of AA TAC by Fe-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the most common and representative antioxidant in daily life, AA content was also considered an important indicator of fruit freshness and nutritional quality (Roshanak, Rahimmalek, & Goli, 2016). Therefore, the TAC analysis represented by AA revealed its potential application value. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity recovery of Fe-CDs depended on the concentration in AA. Therefore, it could be directly used to detect AA, and fluorescence increased with the increase of AA concentration. The linear equation was y = 0.0107× + 0.9947 (R2 = 0.9927, n = 11), and the linear concentration was in the range of 4–40 μM (Fig. 5B). In addition, the detection limit (LOD) was 2.1 μM. Not only that, Fe-CDs showed specific POD-like activity, and AA inhibited the oxidation of TMB. With the gradual increase in AA concentration, the absorbance of the oxTMB system gradually decreased as shown in Fig. 5D. By calculating the absorbance value at 650 nm and performing linear fitting (Fig. 5E), Fe-CDs were found to have good linear correlation for AA detection, with a detection range of 6.3–150 μM, a linear equation of y = −0.0036× + 1.0663 (R2 = 0.9999, n = 7), and an LOD of 0.4 μM. Meanwhile, the color of the system solution changed from dark blue to light blue with increasing AA concentration (Fig. S16). The color change was further captured using a smartphone and then converted into RGB values. It showed a good linear response to B values in the range and the obtained linear equation was y = 0.5237× + 32.6570 (R2 = 0.9987, n = 7), with a detection range of 10–150 μM. Compared with other similar methods based on nanozyme or CDs in Table S2, Fe-CDs carbon dots nanozymes we prepared showed a wider detection range and a considerable LOD for AA detection. Furthermore, Fe-CDs-based fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array provided a more ideal method for selective recognition of various antioxidants.

The reliability and practicality of the AA TAC detection method had also been fully evaluated through the standard addition method. Different concentrations of AA were fitted into diluted prickly pear juice, and then quantitatively determined based on the linearity of the aforementioned fluorescence/colorimetric method. As shown in Tables S3 and S4, the recovery rate of the fluorescence sensor ranged from 94.2 % to 106.6 %, with an RSD range of 1.6 % to 6.8 %. While the recovery rate of the colorimetric sensor was within the range of 95.9 %–96.6 %, the RSD% was less than 5.2 %. This high recovery rate indicated that Fe-CDs had broad development prospects in the accurate and simple detection of TAC content in fruit juice. Usually, there are many food additives in fruit juice; thus, eliminating the interference of food additives is a prerequisite for AA detection. A total of 13 different amino acids and 6 interfering ions were selected as interfering substances for detecting AA. As shown in Figs. 5C and 5F, the relative fluorescence or absorbance intensity of almost all interfering substances remained unchanged (within the range of 0.8–1.1). This indicated that Fe-CDs still maintained good specificity for AA detection and had potential practical application value. Catalytic metal ions (such as Cu2+, Ag+) might be of great significance for fluorescence/colorimetric detection, as these ions often participate in redox reactions or fluorescence quenching, resulting in inaccurate signals. The effects of Cu2+ and Ag+ on dual-mode detection based on Fe-CDs were investigated in Fig. S17. The experimental results demonstrated that Cu2+ significantly catalyzed the oxidation of TMB, resulting in amplified signals. Meanwhile, the effects of Cu2+ on fluorescence detection and Ag+ on colorimetric and fluorescence signals were both less than 10 %. Based on this, a strategy of eliminating interference could be adopted in the pretreatment process of the sample to reduce the influence of Cu2+ on the detection results. For example, using EDTA to chelate Cu2+ to block redox activity or using chelating resins (such as iminodiacetate columns) to preremove Cu2+. Further selective experiments were conducted using xanthine (XMP), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose, galactose, fructose, and maltose as representative interfering substances of food additives or cellular components. As shown in Fig. S18, the impact on fluorescence and absorbance intensities was less than 10 % and 17 %, indicating the satisfactory performance of the dual-mode sensor based on Fe-CDs in AA detection with high specificity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging GSH content and biocompatibility study in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione is a natural and active antioxidant that has multiple physiological functions in the human body. It helps maintain normal redox physiological responses. Abnormal GSH levels are associated with many diseases (Tan, Yin, Ma, Zhang, Pan, Tan et al., 2023). Effective detection of GSH content in cells is also very important. Utilizing the fluorescence imaging performance of Fe-CDs itself, the visualized changes of GSH content in cells were studied by CLSM (Fig. 6A). Fe-CDs entered cells and emitted corresponding fluorescence. When GSH was added to introduce sufficient GSH into cells, the blue fluorescence was brighter than that of the blank control group without GSH. This was consistent with the previous experimental results. After adding NEM to deplete GSH, the previously observed bright blue fluorescence was effectively weakened. Further quantifying the above CLSM image (Fig. 6B), the fluorescence intensity significantly increased by more than 5 times after adding 1 mM GSH. These results indicated that Fe-CDs indeed guided changes in GSH content through fluorescence imaging at the cellular level. Finally, the cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs was evaluated on normal human liver cells (HL-7702 cells), as shown in Fig. 6C. Negligible cytotoxicity (over 90 %) was observed at all the test concentrations (12.5–200 μg/mL). This excellent biocompatibility of Fe-CDs laid a solid foundation for potential food and biological applications.

In this work, we constructed a fluorescence/colorimetric intelligent sensor based on Fe-CDs carbon dots nanozymes. Reasonably designed Fe-CDs not only maintained the fluorescence performance of CDs but also endowed them with nanozyme activity. Subsequently, a sensitive and selective dual-mode sensor array was successfully developed for recognizing seven antioxidants (AA, Cys, TA, GSH, CA, GA, and Na2S). Owing to the rational design, Fe-CDs responded to antioxidants through self-fluorescence and exhibited excellent catalytic activity for TMB oxidation. Due to the different reducing abilities of antioxidants, two independent sensor units showed unique fluorescence/colorimetric response to antioxidants. The fluorescence/colorimetric sensor array had been successfully used for TAC evaluation to identify various fruits (sea-buckthorn, apple, mango, pineapple, and orange). More importantly, Fe-CDs had been successfully used for the qualitative and quantitative detection of AA TAC or GSH in real foods and cells. The results suggest that dual-mode sensor has the potential to be widely applied in food science, biomedical, and environmental analysis. In summary, this study, we developed a rational construction strategy for carbon dots nanozymes with good fluorescence/colorimetric sensing. This work not only provides valuable insights for the rational design of multifunctional nanozymes, but also demonstrates their enormous potential for practical applications in food/biological analysis scenarios.
